authors: Bicknell, R. D. C. et al.
title: Predatory drilling and the oldest-known evolutionary arms race in Lapworthella fasciculata
note: ~517 Ma, South Australia; >200 shells; shell-wall thickening tracks rise in perforation frequency. Oldest DOCUMENTED hard-shell arms race — not the origin of predation.

authors: WWF / Zoological Society of London (Living Planet Index)
title: Living Planet Report 2024
note: 73% average decline across 34,836 monitored vertebrate populations, 1970-2020. Average of population rates of change, NOT proportion of animals or populations lost; ~half of tracked populations stable or increasing.

authors: Ceballos, G.; Ehrlich, P. R.; Dirzo, R.
title: Biological annihilation via the ongoing sixth mass extinction signaled by vertebrate population losses and declines
note: Elevated extinction/decline rates; whether the '6th mass extinction' magnitude is reached is definition-dependent and debated.

title: Tiny vampires in ancient seas: evidence for predation via perforation in fossils from the 780-740 million-year-old Chuar Group, Grand Canyon, USA
venue: Proceedings of the Royal Society B
note: Oldest DIRECT fossil evidence for predation on eukaryotes (~740-780 Ma), >200 My before the Cambrian. Drill holes in vase-shaped microfossils, likely by vampyrellid-like predatory protists.

title: The origin of phagocytosis in Earth history
note: Phagocytosis (one cell engulfing/eating another — cellular basis of predation) likely originated ~1.2 Ga or earlier; timing and single-vs-multiple origins debated. LECA age 'early' (>1.6 Ga) vs 'late' (<1.2 Ga) unresolved.

authors: de Nooijer, S.; Holland, B. R.; Penny, D.
title: The Emergence of Predators in Early Life: There was No Garden of Eden
note: COUNTER-POSITION, and IMPORTANT: this is a simulation / plausibility argument that predation arose very early — NOT fossil proof. Cite it as a claim, not established evidence. No actual early predator is documented; the deep fossil record is silent. Do not let it force hedging.
